Title: LSS6 to boost renewable energy capacity, full commercial operation targeted by end-2029
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The government will implement the Large Scale Solar 6 (LSS6) programme as part of its commitment to accelerating the nation's energy transition and expanding renewable energy (RE) generation capacity, with projects scheduled to be commissioned in phases and targeted to achieve full commercial operation by Dec 31, 2029.
The Energy Transition and Water Transformation Ministry (Petra) said the programme will offer a total quota of 2,500 megawatts (MW) of solar capacity together with 1,250MW of Battery Energy Storage System (BESS), as well as 150MW of solar capacity dedicated to Bumiputera companies in Peninsular Malaysia.
"The implementation of LSS6 builds on the success of previous LSS programmes, which have strengthened the domestic solar industry while lowering electricity generation costs through a transparent, market-based bidding mechanism.
"To encourage broader industry participation, LSS6 will be implemented through three packages. Package 1 comprises an open tender for all developers covering 2,200MW of solar capacity and 1,100MW of BESS capacity.
Package 2 is an open tender for Bumiputera companies involving 300MW of solar capacity and 150MW of BESS, while Package 3 comprises 150MW of Bumiputera solar programme without BESS," it said in a statement today.
The ministry said several policy enhancements have also been introduced to ensure that capacity allocations are implemented fairly, transparently and competitively.
These include the development of large-scale solar plants integrated with BESS to enhance the stability, flexibility and reliability of the national electricity supply system, as well as giving priority to bidders using domestically manufactured photovoltaic solar modules.
It added that project development will focus on strategic areas with strong electricity demand growth, particularly in the southern region of Peninsular Malaysia, to optimise generation planning and the national electricity transmission system.
The ministry said the implementation of LSS6 is expected to attract between RM13 billion and RM15 billion in private investment and create between 15,000 and 20,000 jobs during the development and construction phases.
"In addition, the programme is projected to reduce carbon dioxide (CO₂) emissions by approximately 2.6 million tonnes annually, thereby supporting the country's commitment to a low-carbon energy transition and sustainable development goals," the statement said.
To encourage the participation of small Bumiputera companies in the renewable energy industry, Petra said a special programme under Package 3 will be implemented separately without BESS installation, with smaller-scale solar projects between 10MW and 30MW.
"The programme is expected to generate investment opportunities worth nearly RM500 million for Bumiputera companies," it said.
It added that the Request for Proposal (RFP) documents for Packages 1 and 2 will be available from July 27 to Aug 7, while those for Package 3 will be available from Aug 17 to 28, at the Energy Commission headquarters here.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Burnham faces 'harder test' to mirror Manchester's revival
Article:
WHEN Andy Burnham left Britain's parliament in 2017 to become Greater Manchester's first elected mayor, few would have predicted his return within a decade to be prime minister.
A government minister in the 2000s who twice lost centre-left Labour Party leadership contests, Burnham, 56, found political rejuvenation and the perfect springboard for his ambition in the thriving northwest English city-region.
"He played a good hand well," Henri Murison, chief executive at The Northern Powerhouse Partnership think tank, said of Burnham's mayoral tenure.
"The question is... how does he play a bad one?" he asked, referring to Britain's gloomier national picture of high debt, squeezed finances, sluggish growth and poor productivity.
On Friday, Burnham will vanquish the ghosts of his past to become ruling Labour's new leader. He will replace outgoing Prime Minister Keir Starmer at 10 Downing Street on Monday.
He has vowed to devolve powers to other cities and create a "No. 10 North" based in Manchester to ensure regions outside the British capital are not neglected.
But Burnham's apparent bid to emulate Manchester's decades-spanning transformation from post-industrial wasteland to economic success story may not survive contact with Britain's rigid national governing culture.
"This is a harder test than he had in Greater Manchester," said Murison.
Some question whether Burnham even played a meaningful role in the city-region's revival, and has the skillset to deliver seismic change.
Greater Manchester – home to three million people – is several decades into a long-term project to boost public and private sector investment through entrepreneurial local governance and various data-led strategies, collaborations and innovations.
Home to two world-famous football teams, five major higher education institutions and a highly profitable airport part-owned by the regional government, its burgeoning skyline is testament to its turnaround under so-called "Manchesterism."
Under 2016 reforms that created the mayoralty, Burnham headed the Greater Manchester Combined Authority but shared power in devolved policy areas like transport with the elected leaders of the city-region's 10 boroughs.
That meant he was mostly just "one of 11 votes" and more like "the captain of a football team," noted journalist and author Andy Spinoza, whose 2023 book "Manchester Unspun" charts its 40-year resurrection.
"He's had to be collaborative, and people talk about that as being his superpower when really, it's a basic prerequisite of the job."
[[nid:1490166]]
Less than 1,000 people lived in the city centre in the early 1990s, compared to more than 100,000 now, explained Philip McCann, professor at the Manchester-based Productivity Institute.
"Walk around... the changes are phenomenal," he told AFP.
With Manchester, he and others credit the "visionary leadership" of Howard Bernstein, the city council's unelected chief executive from 1998 to 2017, and Richard Leese, its elected leader for 25 years from 1996.
McCann argued Burnham has been "both a custodian and an accelerant" of their agenda.
Working in Greater Manchester required power-sharing and "long-term thinking", Leese told AFP.
"That's not the way that Westminster works," he added.
"But not only did he learn a new way of working, he now wants to take that new way of working and try and apply that to a central government that's frankly been dysfunctional for decades."
For Spinoza and others, Burnham's achievements have been overstated.
"The part that he's played is both being in the right place at the right time," he said. "He's an agile politician who's seen this opportunity and Manchester's given it to him."
Burnham's most visible policy success was bringing the previously fragmented, privately run bus system back under public control within an integrated transport network.
He is also credited with trying to broaden economic activity geographically.
"His political skill has created this kind of momentum and legitimacy around a Greater Manchester idea," Joshi Herrmann, founder of local journalism start-up Mill Media, told AFP.
However, Burnham took office vowing to end rough sleeping in the city by 2020 and failed.
Matthew Johnson, homeless charity Lifeshare's chairman, lauded him for raising the problem's profile and showing "genuine care."
But he noted "homelessness remains a huge issue" and views the failure as symbolic of something bigger.
"We dreamt of becoming like London, and that's what we've managed to do – we've created a more unequal city space."
Burnham endured other setbacks, including attempting to impose a clean air zone on drivers that ended up a costly failure.
McCann is nonetheless "delighted" the ex-mayor is taking power after having "lived and breathed" devolution during a period in which the UK government has become increasingly open to it.
"It almost feels like this is the real big bang now," he said. "I am optimistic."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK regulator to probe TikTok on child safety measures
Article:
LONDON: Britain's media regulator on Thursday launched a probe into TikTok to understand whether its UK unit failed or is failing to protect children from harmful content.
The probe comes a month after the government imposed a blanket ban on social media for users under the age of 16 and imposed restrictions on gaming and live-streaming platforms.
Ofcom will probe whether the platform has measures to assess if a particular user is a child and adequate systems and processes to prevent children from viewing harmful content.
In May, the regulator said TikTok had failed to set out meaningful steps to protect British children from harmful online content.
The opening of an investigation does not mean that Ofcom has reached any conclusion about whether the provider has breached its duties, Ofcom said.
TikTok did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PAC dissatisfied with response to KL land development recommendations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Parliamentary Public Accounts Committee (PAC) has expressed dissatisfaction with the government's responses to its recommendations on Kuala Lumpur land development, urging it to demonstrate genuine political will.
PAC chairman Datuk Wira Mas Ermieyati Samsudin said the committee wants the government to reconsider its recommendations more seriously to show sincerity in resolving governance failures related to land management and development in the capital.
Speaking at a press conference in Parliament today, she said the committee conducted a follow-up proceeding on July 15 to assess responses to the recommendations in the PAC Statement on Kuala Lumpur land development, which was tabled in the Dewan Rakyat on Aug 25 last year.
Mas Ermieyati said the absence of meaningful engagement with members of parliament was the most concerning gap identified.
She noted that although MPs have access to Kuala Lumpur City Hall's One Stop Centre system, land approval processes under the Kuala Lumpur Federal Territory Land Working Committee's (JKTWPKL) jurisdiction are still carried out without substantive involvement of MPs, making it difficult for them to explain development matters to their constituents.
On the chairmanship of JKTWPKL, the PAC stressed that the post should be returned to a minister to strengthen democratic accountability, noting that the matter remains under cabinet consideration.
The PAC also noted that a proposed legislative amendment to establish a DBKL Council Member System could not be implemented, which the committee fears will undermine the check-and-balance agenda.
Mas Ermieyati said relying solely on internal administrative reforms is insufficient to guarantee greater organisational transparency without structural change within DBKL.
"PAC is concerned that the voice of the people on land development continues to be marginalised, thereby undermining overall public welfare," she said, adding that critical policy recommendations were repeatedly being deferred to Cabinet consideration rather than acted upon.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man fined RM4,000 over false TikTok post on diesel shipment claim
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A man was fined RM4,000, in default six months' jail, by the Sessions Court here today for posting false content on TikTok about an alleged diesel shipment to the Philippines.
The accused, Shahuddin Gowana, 51, pleaded guilty after the charge was read before Judge Monica Linsua.
According to the charge, Shahuddin knowingly used the TikTok account "@izzyderabbit" to initiate the transmission of a communication containing false content with the intention of annoying others.
The video was viewed at about 8.57am on Apr 15, 2026, at Level 8, MCMC Tower 1, Jalan IMPACT, Cyber 6, Cyberjaya, Sepang, Selangor.
The offence was allegedly committed at about 2pm on Apr 14, 2026, at Universiti Utama Condominium, Jalan Kayu Madang, Telipok.
He was charged under Section 233(1)(a) of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, punishable under Section 233(2) of the same Act, which carries a fine of up to RM500,000, imprisonment for up to two years, or both, as well as a further fine of RM5,000 for each day the offence continues after conviction.
According to the facts of the case, the three-minute-and-42-second video alleged, among other things, that the Malaysian government was dishonest and stated that anyone who believed the government's denial regarding the alleged shipment of diesel drums to the Philippines was foolish.
The accused's statements in the video also appeared to suggest that reports issued by the Philippines were genuine and accurate.
The facts stated that the contents of the video were false, misleading and unverified.
The allegation concerning the shipment of diesel to the Philippines was also denied by the National Economic Action Council, which clarified that the shipment belonged to an international trading company.
The communication was transmitted with the intention of annoying others, including by causing members of the public to believe false information, potentially creating confusion, misunderstanding, hatred or anger towards the Malaysian government, particularly Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
The communication also had the potential to undermine public confidence in the country's economic stability and the government's administration.
In mitigation, Shahuddin, who was unrepresented, asked the court for a lenient sentence, citing his guilty plea, which saved judicial time.
The accused said he worked as an e-hailing driver and claimed to be a content creator. He also told the court that he suffered from health problems and was the sole breadwinner supporting his wife, seven children, four of whom are still in school, as well as his elderly mother.
Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission prosecuting officer Nur Nazhzilah Mohammad Hashim, among others, urged the court to impose a deterrent sentence, submitting that the accused had abused freedom of speech by disseminating false content.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: iCents bags RM12.9mil contract to renovate factory, office in Klang Valley
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: iCents Group Holdings Bhd's wholly-owned VC Engineering Sdn Bhd has secured a RM12.9 million contract to undertake renovation works for a factory, warehouse and office in the Klang Valley.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the company said VC Engineering had accepted a letter of award from the Malaysian subsidiary of a Netherlands-based technology company.
The Dutch firm specialises in semiconductor, medical and life sciences, as well as infrastructure and energy solutions.
"The total contract value of the letter of award amount is RM12.9 million, exclusive of sales and service tax.
"The scope of works comprises, among others, supply, delivery and installation of building works, internal finishes, mechanical, electrical, extra low voltage system, plumbing sanitary, fire fighting systems and testing commissioning works," iCents said.
The project is scheduled for completion within four months.
iCents executive director Foo Slang Leng said the contract broadens its project portfolio.
It also underscores customers' confidence in the company's ability to deliver integrated engineering and construction solutions for technology-driven industries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Police must outpace evolving crime through AI, digitalisation, says deputy IGP
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The police force must stay ahead of evolving crime by harnessing digitalisation, data analytics and artificial intelligence (AI) to deliver more efficient and effective policing services.
Deputy Inspector-General of Police Tan Sri Ayob Khan Mydin Pitchay said policing today faced increasingly complex threats, including cybercrime, online scams, AI manipulation, deepfake technology and various forms of transnational crime.
He said the force must stay ahead of criminal trends by embracing technology and innovation to strengthen policing capabilities.
"Smart Policing is not merely about adopting modern technology. It also encompasses a culture of data-driven decision-making, digital service delivery and operational management that is more responsive, accurate and upholds integrity," he said when opening the Police Innovation Day 2026 celebration here today.
Ayob said he was proud of the innovation initiatives driven through research and development efforts to leverage digital technology and AI-based applications, in line with the transformation agenda of the police and the Home Ministry.
He said innovation was a key driver in transforming the police into a modern, professional and resilient organisation capable of delivering better services to the public.
"Every idea generated should translate into solutions that improve operational effectiveness, strengthen service delivery and add value to the organisation," he said.
Ayob urged police personnel to make innovation part of their daily work culture rather than limiting it to competitions or special projects.
He also called on officers to continue generating new ideas, challenge existing work practices and embrace emerging technologies ethically.
Ayob said stronger collaboration with government agencies, universities, industry players and strategic partners was crucial to ensure expertise, technology and resources could be fully utilised to develop high-impact innovations.
He said Police Innovation Day served as an important platform to demonstrate the force's commitment to supporting the government's public sector reform agenda through innovation and digital transformation.
The event, themed "Digitalisation Driving Smart Policing", featured innovative projects developed by various police contingents and departments aimed at improving operational efficiency and public service delivery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia higher at midday
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia ended the morning session higher, backed by continuous buying in heavyweights and mid-capitalisation stocks.
At 12.30 pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) rose 10.32 points to 1,725.30 from Wednesday's close of 1,713.76.
The FBM KLCI, which opened  3.0 points higher at 1,716.76, moved between 1,716.76 and 1,723.23 during the morning trading session.
However, on the broader market, losers surpassed gainers 469 to 440, while 497 counters were unchanged, 1,329 untraded, and 12 suspended.
Turnover stood at 1.99 billion units worth RM1.28 billion.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of Equity Research Thong Pak Leng said as of today, he anticipates the index to hover within the 1,710-1,720 range.
Meanwhile, Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) said despite improving technical signals and renewed foreign inflows supporting further upside, near-term volatility is likely to persist amid lingering West Asia tensions, global supply chain risks and a hawkish US Federal Reserve stance.
Among the heavyweights, Malayan Banking Bhd rose six sen to RM11.02, Public Bank Bhd added 12 sen to RM5.17, CIMB Group Holdings Bhd was one sen firmer at RM7.72, while Tenaga Nasional Bhd fell eight sen to RM14.30 and IHH Healthcare Bhd slid seven sen to RM8.55.
As for the active stocks, NationGate Holdings Bhd gained 18 sen to RM1.15, VS Industry Bhd increased two sen to 24 sen, Dagang NeXchange Bhd rose three sen to 47.5 sen, while Tanco Holdings Bhd lost 1.5 sen to 30 sen and Thriven Global Bhd was down two sen to 10 sen.
Of the top gainers, Petronas Dagangan Bhd increased 32 sen to RM19.64, Fraser & Neave Holdings Bhd rose 24 sen to RM29.34, United Plantations Bhd grew 20 sen to RM34.90, while IOI Properties Group Bhd firmed 15 sen to RM3.92.
Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd led the losers list, falling 82 sen to RM44.92, while UMS Integration Ltd lost 30 sen to RM8.01, Kluang Rubber Co (Malaya) Bhd shed 17 sen to RM6.28, Panasonic Manufacturing Malaysia Bhd was 13 sen weaker at RM5.35, and Apollo Food Holdings Bhd eased 12 sen to RM5.73.
Among the broader indices, the FBM Emas Index rose by 67.60 points to 12,717.34, and the FBMT 100 Index gained 67.47 points to 12,547.99.
The FBM Emas Shariah Index racked up 54.72 points to 12,557.51, the FBM 70 Index increased 25.88 points to 17,911.66, while the FBM ACE Index narrowed 3.93 points to 4,907.82.
Sector-wise, the Financial Services Index surged 138.79 points to 20,293.19, the Industrial Products and Services Index firmed 1.70 points to 188.45, and the Plantation Index strengthened 43.48 points to 9,484.44, while the Energy Index edged down 0.83 of a point to 769.33.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family seeks RM21,500 for 4-year-old's pacemaker treatment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Four-year-old Wong Yong Cing has undergone medical procedures since infancy for a congenital heart condition that now requires a pacemaker.
Her family is seeking RM21,500 for a dual-chamber pacemaker after doctors diagnosed her with a ventricular septal defect (VSD), a condition involving a hole in the wall separating the heart's lower chambers.
Born with Down Syndrome, Wong Yong Cing was diagnosed with the condition shortly after birth. Her father, Wong Kin Neng, said he and his wife did not undergo certain prenatal screening tests during the pregnancy due to financial constraints.
"First, the doctor told us she had Down Syndrome. Then they found out she had a hole in her heart and transferred her to Sultan Idris Shah Hospital (HSIS) for treatment," he said.
Wong said his daughter was born with a 4mm hole in her heart and had undergone several procedures since infancy.
"When she was two months old and weighed about 5kg, she underwent surgery to tie the blood vessel (pulmonary artery banding) because she was still too small for a major operation.
"We waited for about one to three years before doctors said she was ready for the next operation," he said.
Congenital heart surgery medical officer at HSIS, Dr Hazwan Hairudin, said Wong Yong Ching was suffering from congenital heart disease involving a VSD.
"In simple terms, there is a hole between the right and left ventricles of the heart. This condition has existed since birth.
"After the operation, her heart rhythm became irregular due to a heart block, which required the insertion of a pacemaker," he said.
He said the pacemaker was a small battery-powered device that provides electrical stimulation to help regulate the heartbeat.
"If the pacemaker is not inserted, the heart may stop beating and it could be life-threatening," he said.
Dr Hazwan said the device would last about seven to eight years before requiring replacement and Wong Yong Ching would need to use it for the rest of her life.
Wong said the financial burden had been difficult for the family as he frequently had to take leave from work to bring his daughter for medical appointments at HSIS.
"I am a lorry driver and earn about RM3,600. I have two children, with my eldest being 11 years old and Wong Yong Ching the youngest.
"It is not enough to cover all the costs. I often take leave on Mondays and Thursdays to bring her to the hospital," he said.
Wong said his wife, who is Vietnamese, also faced difficulties communicating with medical staff during hospital visits, requiring him to accompany her and their daughter.
He said his biggest hope was for Wong Yong Ching to eventually be able to eat normally without relying on a feeding tube.
"I am happy that there are people willing to help," he said.
The cost of Wong Yong Ching's dual-chamber pacemaker was assessed by the Medical Social Work Department, which found that the family was unable to bear the expenses.
The case has been referred to the Media Prima Humanitarian Fund to raise public contributions.
Members of the public who wish to contribute can donate to the Media Prima-NSTP Humanitarian Fund through its Bank Muamalat account (1401 0008 8427 18).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL rolls out 16 reforms after poor corruption ranking score
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: DBKL's poor score in last year's Public Service Corruption Ranking has prompted 16 governance and administrative reforms to be implemented within six months, the Dewan Rakyat was told today.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said the reforms were also driven by recommendations from a study by the International Islamic University Malaysia (IIUM).
She said DBKL scored just 0.08 per cent out of a possible five per cent in the ranking last year, highlighting the need for improvements in governance and administration.
"In line with the recommendations in the IIUM study and in acknowledging the reality of the score obtained, DBKL is implementing 16 governance and administration reform initiatives within the next six months," she said during minister's question time in the Dewan Rakyat.
Yeoh was responding to a question from Tan Kok Wai (PH-Cheras) on governance reforms arising from a feasibility study of the Federal Capital Act.
Among the key reforms is the implementation of five advisories from the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), which identified procedural loopholes in five areas that could create room for misconduct.
These involve the management of studio broadcasting content publication projects, allocation of Ramadan and Aidilfitri bazaar sites, management of multiple business licence contracts, governance of the 2023 Malaysian Statutory Bodies Sports Association Championship (Sukanun), and rental collection management for people's housing and public housing projects.
The Special One-Stop Centre Committee has also been abolished to ensure the separation of powers and prevent political interference in development decisions.
On transparency, Hannah said access to the OSC 3.0 Plus development application portal had been opened to members of parliament, stakeholders and the general public after MPs complained they were unable to obtain complete information on approved developments in their constituencies.
The mayor's authority to approve contributions without limit has also been curtailed, with a ceiling of RM3,000 now in place. Any contribution exceeding that amount must be brought before the Senior Management Committee for discussion and approval.
Two further reforms give MPs a direct role in city governance: five budget monitoring committees have been established and will be chaired by MPs, while KL MPs will now also chair joint consultation meetings with DBKL executive directors to ensure more inclusive decision-making.
Hannah said the reforms flowed from IIUM engagement sessions held in March with KL MPs and DBKL management to gather views on governance, administrative structure, decision-making, service delivery, enforcement and accountability under the framework of the Federal Capital Act.
The study identified four broad areas for reform covering internal procedures, the empowerment of committees, the strengthening of the Advisory Board, and the operation of the Federal Territories Ministers' Council.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China's rare earth curbs endanger US$6.5 trillion of Western industry, IEA says
Article:
LONDON: The full implementation of China's rare earth export restrictions could put US$6.5 trillion of downstream production outside the country at risk, the International Energy Agency warned on Thursday.
China, the world's largest producer of rare earths, expanded export controls in October last year to cover additional materials and introduced new licensing requirements, but later agreed to delay implementation for a year.
Rare earths are a group of 17 metals used in small quantities, but essential to products ranging from cars and aircraft to electronics and weapons systems.
If the controls take full effect, about US$6.5 trillion of production across the automotive, high-tech, defence and energy sectors could be exposed to supply disruptions, the IEA said in its Global Critical Minerals Outlook report.
The U.S. and Europe would account for nearly half of the economic impact, the report added.
"Our latest analysis shows that vast amounts of economic value depend on relatively small volumes of critical minerals, whose supply chains remain highly concentrated and are therefore vulnerable," IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol said.
The agency also warned of risks from China's planned export controls on graphite, a key material used in electric vehicle batteries, which were announced at the same time and later postponed.
Full implementation of the graphite controls could put about US$300 billion of downstream production outside China at risk, the report said. China accounts for more than 90 per cent of global processed graphite output.
Western governments have been trying to build alternative critical mineral supply chains. The IEA said public financing commitments for new projects more than quadrupled between 2023 and 2025 to US$65 billion.
New rare earth refining projects in the US and Malaysia reduced China's share of the global market to 85 per cent last year from 90 per cent in 2023, the agency said.
If planned projects proceed on schedule, that share could fall to 70 per cent by 2035.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman charged with murder of newborn baby in Subang Jaya
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A supermarket saleswoman was charged at the magistrate's court today with murdering her newborn baby last week.
Siti Nur Wahida Zainudin, who turned 23 on Tuesday, was accused of committing the offence between 5am and 5.30pm on July 7 at an apartment unit in USJ 14, Subang Jaya.
She appeared in court dressed in a dark blue outfit and handcuffed.
She told magistrate Ameera Mastura Khamis that she understood the charge after it was read out to her.
She nodded when the court interpreter asked if she understood the nature of the charge and the punishment for the offence.
No plea was recorded as cases involving murder fall under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
The charge, under Section 302 of the Penal Code, provides for the death penalty or a jail term of between 30 and 40 years and, if not sentenced to death, not fewer than 12 strokes of the rotan.
Siti Nur Wahida, who remained expressionless throughout the proceedings, was unrepresented.
Deputy public prosecutor Nurul Akhilah Rosmi did not offer bail as the offence is non-bailable.
She asked the court to set a mention date for the appointment of a lawyer and the submission of relevant documents, including the post-mortem report.
Ameera denied bail and fixed Oct 23 for mention.
Siti Nur Wahida's elder sister, who was present in the public gallery, broke down in tears during the proceedings.
She pleaded with the media to blur her sister's face in photographs, saying Siti Nur Wahida should be presumed innocent until proven guilty to protect their parents in the kampung from being affected by the case.
After the proceedings, Siti Nur Wahida was heard telling her sister that she wanted to go home.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: When suspicion becomes the default response
Article:
THE recent hypermarket incident that went viral on social media was uncomfortable to watch.
A pregnant woman was accused of shoplifting and made to stand for 20 minutes while the situation was investigated. The hypermarket chain later apologised, saying the way the matter was handled did not meet the standards expected of its customers.
Understandably, much of the conversation centred around customer service, the treatment of a vulnerable shopper, and whether the employee had acted appropriately.
But perhaps there is another uncomfortable question worth asking: How did we get to a point where suspicion has become such a natural response in everyday transactions?
Trust, after all, does not disappear overnight. It is slowly worn down by countless small acts that many of us dismiss as harmless.
You don't need to witness a crime to understand why trust is in short supply these days.
Just stand in the fruit aisle of a supermarket long enough and you may see it.
Someone opens a punnet of blueberries, picks out the larger ones from another container to "improve" theirs, then quietly places the half-empty punnet back on the shelf for someone else to buy.
No confrontation. No argument. No obvious victim standing there to complain.
It's a tiny act, almost laughably insignificant. Yet it says something profound about us.
The same happens when someone takes a few grapes from one bag to make another look fuller, reserves a parking space by standing in it, cuts a queue because they are "only buying one item", or treats self-checkout counters as an opportunity rather than a responsibility.
Each act may seem insignificant. A few blueberries. A few minutes saved. A small advantage gained.
But collectively, these behaviours create a culture where everyone becomes a little more suspicious of everyone else.
The hidden cost of these small selfish acts is not measured in ringgit and cents. It is measured in the trust we lose with one another.
We often think kiasu behaviour costs only a few ringgit. In reality, it costs something far more difficult to replace: the simple belief that the stranger standing opposite us is acting in good faith.
There is nothing wrong with wanting value for money, getting a good bargain, or making smart choices. But when the desire to gain a small advantage comes at someone else's expense, it stops being about being savvy. It becomes about shifting the cost of our choices onto others. At that point, it is simply irresponsible behaviour.
The hypermarket incident was not only about one employee's actions.
It also highlighted what happens when suspicion becomes the default lens through which ordinary interactions are viewed. Retail workers are expected to prevent theft. Customers expect to be treated with dignity. When confidence between both sides breaks down, everyone loses.
Perhaps this is why the hypermarket case resonates so strongly. Many people are not only reacting to one employee's actions. They are reacting to a deeper feeling that we live in a society where everyone is watching everyone else — because too many people have learned that someone will always try to exploit the benefit of the doubt.
We often complain about being distrusted.
But trust is expensive to maintain when everyday actions keep proving that suspicion may sometimes be justified.
Every time someone tops up their blueberries from another punnet, nobody calls the police. Nobody posts it on social media. Nobody goes viral.
Yet every small act sends a quiet message to the next person — whether they are a shopper, cashier, or security guard — that perhaps trust is no longer the safest assumption.
The writer is a journalist with the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: H5N1 bird flu could take hold in New Zealand within months
Article:
WELLINGTON: A newly detected strain of H5N1 avian influenza is likely to become endemic in New Zealand within months, after the country recorded its first case in a wild seabird, a senior veterinary official said on Thursday, reported Xinhua.
Ministry for Primary Industries' Chief Veterinary Officer Mary van Andel told Radio New Zealand (RNZ) that eradication would be unlikely if the virus establishes in wildlife.
The H5 virus was confirmed Wednesday in a seabird found on Petone Beach of the capital Wellington, marking New Zealand's first detection amid a global outbreak that has spread widely among wild birds.
"New Zealand's isolation will not keep out this deadly bird virus," Professor Dianne Brunton at the University of Auckland School of Biological Sciences said, adding although there is no evidence of mass wildlife deaths or transmission between New Zealand birds, H5N1 is likely to become established in time.
The virus could spill from birds to humans, but such cases are rare, van Andel said, adding that food safety is unaffected.
Professor Jemma Geoghegan, a virologist at the University of Otago, said New Zealand had been preparing for the virus to arrive after its recent spread to Australia and globally.
Rapid testing, genomic sequencing and close monitoring of wild birds will be essential, said Geoghegan.
The Department of Conservation has begun vaccinating 300 core breeding birds from five of New Zealand's most endangered species, including kakapo and takahe, as part of preparedness efforts.
Massey University epidemiologist Nigel French told RNZ that small, endangered populations, including the critically rare fairy tern, could face extinction if the virus spreads.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police: Military cosplay outfits may breach the law if it misleads public
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Wearing military uniforms and carrying imitation weapons at cosplay events could land participants in trouble with the law if they mislead the public or violate weapons regulations.
Bukit Aman Crime Prevention and Community Safety Department director Datuk Noor Hisam Nordin said while wearing uniforms resembling security forces, including foreign soldiers, is not automatically an offence, it becomes one if done to mislead the public into believing the individual is an actual military member.
Such cases are investigated under Section 140 of the Penal Code.
"If the costume is accompanied by imitation weapon props, such as rifles, replica pistols or airsoft guns without a valid licence, the individual may be investigated under Section 36 of the Arms Act 1960.
"Participants must also pay attention to the ban on carrying imitation firearms that resemble real weapons, and scheduled sharp weapons including the keris, machetes, spears, axes, brass knuckles and samurai swords, even if they are replicas," he said.
Noor Hisam said that during the first six months of this year, one investigation paper was opened under Section 140 of the Penal Code following an incident in Gombak, Selangor, where a viral video showed a man wearing clothing resembling security personnel while carrying a suspected imitation weapon. No arrests were made in that case.
However, under Section 36 of Act 206, a total of 63 investigation papers were opened, resulting in 95 arrests during the same period.
"Ignorance of the law cannot be used as an excuse to avoid legal action," Noor Hisam said, advising participants and organisers to ensure costumes and props do not violate the law.
He encouraged event organisers to provide clear guidelines on prohibited costumes and accessories at registration areas to ensure a safe, orderly, and law-abiding environment without restricting creativity.
An event organiser known only as Lman said such events focus on anime, manga, and video games rather than military attire, noting that participants dressed in wartime uniforms could give the public the wrong impression.
Lman said organisers enforce compliance by prohibiting sharp objects or replica weapons that pose security risks.
"We check the accessories participants bring, and if they do not comply with the conditions, they may be asked to leave the venue or change their clothing," Lman said.
Meanwhile, a 19-year-old private university student known as Daniel said his military uniform, bought online, was worn out of an interest in history and historical reenactment rather than to promote any ideology.
"I see this clothing as part of history. I wear it out of interest, not to support an ideology, and I only wear it in the event area," Daniel said.
A private sector employee identified as Muiz, 27, said he and a group of individuals have been active in the historical reenactment community for two years and use cosplay events to interact with others who share the hobby.
Another participant, San, 27, noted that his replica weapons were made of wood and plastic, adding that participants generally adhere to the guidelines set by event organisers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Time Energy completes first phase of homeowner solar rewards programme
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Time Energy Sdn Bhd has completed the first phase of its Green Rewards Programme, a community initiative that allows households with solar panels to earn renewable energy certificates (RECs).
Time Energy is the renewable energy arm of Time dotCom Bhd.
RECs are issued for electricity generated from renewable sources such as solar power, with one REC representing one megawatt-hour of renewable electricity produced.
Companies can buy RECs to help meet their sustainability goals, particularly when they are unable to generate enough renewable electricity at their own facilities.
While solar RECs in Malaysia are commonly sourced from utility-scale renewable energy projects, the company said the Green Rewards Programme allows corporate buyers to source community-generated solar RECs from residential rooftops instead.
This supports clean energy generated by Malaysian homes while reducing the
need for additional land to be repurposed for solar farms.
"We are glad to have found a way to connect homeowners to a market that would otherwise be difficult for them to access individually," said Time Energy chief executive officer (CEO) Arjun Arasu.
While the current value of RECs is influenced by developments in environmental, social and governance, and energy markets, Arjun said contribution from homeowners deserves to be recognised.
"As the market develops, we hope this recognition translates into greater value," he added
Individually, each home generates too little solar electricity to participate directly in the REC market.
Through the Green Rewards Programme, Time Energy combines renewable energy generated by participating households, converts it into RECs, sells them, and shares the proceeds with homeowners.
This gives homeowners an additional source of income from the clean energy they generate.
AIMS Group, one of Malaysia's data centre providers, was the first purchaser of RECS generated under the Green Rewards Programme's first phase, which pooled solar electricity produced by participating rent-to-own customers of Time Energy's unit, Emit Solar, in the first half of 2025.
AIMS chief executive officer Chiew Kok Hin said the Green Rewards Programme supports the company's broader sustainability goals.
"This model allows us to connect corporate sustainability action with community-level clean energy. While every effort may start small, lasting impact comes from businesses and communities working together with a shared purpose.
"Through collaboration, we can build on these efforts over time and contribute to a more sustainable future for everyone," said Chiew.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EU accepts Musk's proposed changes to X - for now
Article:
BRUSSELS: Following a multimillion euro fine for breaches of EU rules, Elon Musk's online platform X has satisfied the European Commission with its proposed changes for now, the European Union said on Wednesday, reported German news agency dpa.
The authorised measures will enable greater transparency on the social network and represent an important step in the right direction, the Brussels-based authority announced.
The US company had been ordered to pay €120 million (US$137.7 million) due to a lack of transparency on X under the EU's Digital Services Act (DSA).
The fine centred on what the EU called the misleading authentication of user accounts, indicated by the white verification tick on a blue background.
Following his takeover of the former Twitter platform, Musk rebranded the platform as X and introduced a system whereby all paying subscribers receive a tick or checkmark, rather than restricting it to users verified by the company.
In Brussels' view, this contravened EU rules. Following the fine, X had publicly clarified that the ticks no longer equated to verification. The platform now speaks of a "premium" status.
The European Commission had also accused X of withholding data from researchers and failing to document advertising placements transparently.
X has also promised changes in these areas, which Brussels regulators have provisionally accepted, allowing the platform six months to implement them.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt rules out age-based health checks for drivers aged 70 and above
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government has no plans to impose mandatory health checks based solely on age for private motorists renewing their driving licences from the age of 70.
Deputy Transport Minister Datuk Hasbi Habibollah said the ministry does not require additional medical examinations for the renewal of private driving licences, including for motorists aged 70 and above.
He said research by the Malaysian Institute of Road Safety Research (Miros) found no conclusive evidence that such a policy would significantly reduce road accidents.
"Findings from studies and international analyses show there is no conclusive evidence that introducing mandatory age-based medical examinations can significantly reduce the accident rate," he told the Dewan Rakyat today.
Hasbi said police statistics showed those aged 70 and above accounted for 3.5 per cent of all people involved in road accidents recorded between 2020 and 2025, compared with 69.4 per cent involving those aged between 16 and 40.
However, he said the figures covered everyone involved in accidents, including drivers, motorcyclists, passengers and victims, and did not establish that age was the main contributing factor.
He said restrictions imposed solely on the basis of age could affect senior citizens' mobility and wellbeing, particularly their ability to access healthcare, manage daily affairs and participate in community activities.
"Advancing age does not necessarily mean a person has lost the ability to drive, as each individual's health and capabilities differ. Many are still able to drive safely and responsibly," he said.
Hasbi was responding to Mohd Nazri Abu Hassan (PN-Merbok), who asked whether the government planned to require medical examinations for licence renewals from the age of 70 and whether such a measure would improve road safety without affecting the mobility and wellbeing of senior citizens.
Hasbi said medical examinations are currently mandatory only for new applications and renewals of vocational licences for goods and public service vehicles, regardless of the applicant's age.
He added that existing medical requirements for commercial and public transport drivers are already strict and enforcement should be further strengthened.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hegseth announces testosterone screening for US troops
Article:
WASHINGTON: US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth announced on Wednesday mandatory annual testosterone screening for troops 30 years old or above, an initiative he said was aimed at combatting testosterone deficiency that can undermine health.
Hegseth said the screening could lead to troops being offered testosterone replacement therapy aimed at "ensuring you have the right testosterone levels to operate at your absolute best."
"Because it's well-established science that as we age, testosterone levels often naturally drop," he said in a video message.
The screening would become part of annual testing for troops aged 30 and above, and troops could voluntarily decide whether to accept any recommended hormone replacement treatment if they were diagnosed with low testosterone levels.
Troops below the age of 30 could request to be tested.
The move comes as the US Department of Health and Human Services has moved to ease some restrictions on testosterone replacement therapies, including announcing last month it would seek to remove limits on use ‌in men with age-related low testosterone.
Still, Hegseth's announcement triggered backlash from opposition Democrats, who pointed to his ban on transgender service members who often depended on hormone therapy.
"So now y'all support gender-affirming care?" Democratic Congresswoman Summer Lee said.
"Sounds like gender-affirming care to me," Senator Tammy Duckworth added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New York Times seeks to block subpoenas to reporters over Air Force One reporting
Article:
WASHINGTON: The New York Times has filed a court motion to block federal grand jury subpoenas to three of its journalists over their reporting on President Donald Trump's new Qatari-donated Air Force One, a spokesperson said on Wednesday.
Jay Clayton, the US ​Attorney in Manhattan, issued the subpoenas on Friday, shortly after the New York Times reported that the new Air Force One lacked some of the security features of the older aircraft.
The newspaper's reporters were ordered to appear on Wednesday before a grand jury panel in Manhattan federal court.
"As we set out in our motion, these subpoenas are brought in bad faith to punish The Times for its coverage," David McCraw, senior vice president and deputy general counsel of the New York Times, said in a statement.
"We are going to court to defend our journalists' rights to report freely on the administration and to provide the public with stories that matter."
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told a US Senate panel on Wednesday that the reporters were not targets of an investigation, but the subpoenas were aimed at identifying people who leaked sensitive national security information.
McCraw has asked the court to make public the newspaper's filing, which remains under seal.
The news outlet, he said, "believes that the public has a right to information about this case."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PN rejects Bersatu claim over postponed seat talks
Article:
KOTA BARU: Perikatan Nasional (PN) has dismissed Bersatu's claim that the coalition's Seat Negotiation Committee meeting was postponed indefinitely because Pas wanted to pursue political cooperation with Barisan Nasional (BN).
PN deputy chairman Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man said the postponement was linked to a complaint lodged with the Registrar of Societies (ROS) by a Bersatu member questioning the legitimacy of the party's leadership.
"I understand the decision to postpone the seat negotiation meeting was due to a complaint lodged with the ROS by a Bersatu member.
"The member questioned the legitimacy of Bersatu's leadership, claiming its Supreme Leadership Council did not meet the required quorum.
"PN does not know the actual status of the matter. Therefore, the PN secretary-general requested Bersatu to clarify the situation and obtain confirmation from the ROS.
"However, no explanation has been provided to date," said the Pas deputy president.
Tuan Ibrahim was responding to Bersatu president Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin's statement that the party would field its candidates under its own logo in the 16th Negri Sembilan election.
Muhyiddin said the decision was made last night after the PN Seat Negotiation Committee meeting was postponed indefinitely, with Bersatu excluded from seat allocation discussions.
He said the meeting on July 12 was adjourned after Pas decided to pursue political cooperation with BN.
Muhyiddin said Bersatu maintained that the matter should first be discussed and agreed upon by the PN Supreme Council as it involved the coalition's policies and direction. He said the council had yet to meet and the seat negotiation meeting had not been rescheduled.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN: More than 500 feared dead after boats sink off Myanmar coast
Article:
BANGKOK: Two boats carrying more than 500 people may have capsized off the coast of Myanmar in recent days, UN agencies said on Thursday, as refugees from the war-torn country continue to make perilous maritime journeys in search of safety and better opportunities.
"According to preliminary information, the two vessels departed from Myanmar's Rakhine State in late June carrying mostly Rohingya passengers, reportedly including some who had travelled from refugee camps in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh," the International Organisation for Migration and the UN Refugee Agency said in a joint statement, saying more than 500 were feared dead.
"While the incidents and casualty figures have yet to be officially confirmed, UNHCR and IOM are gravely concerned by the potentially devastating loss of life."
Members of Myanmar's Rohingya Muslim minority for years have risked their lives on flimsy wooden vessels, driven by violence at home and desperate conditions in crowded refugee camps in Bangladesh, hoping to reach safety and opportunity in countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia or Thailand.
With around 250 people onboard, the first boat lost contact shortly after departure and a second vessel carrying around 280 people is believed to have sunk off Myanmar's Ayeyarwady coast on July 8, the UN agencies said.
"These journeys took place outside the regular sailing season, when maritime conditions are typically more hazardous," the statement said.
The agencies said that nearly 300 people are reported to be missing or dead in the Andaman Sea and Bay of Bengal this year, including Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dzulkefly: Type 2 diabetes now affects Malaysians as young as 14
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Type 2 diabetes is now being diagnosed in Malaysians as young as 14, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said.
"We are now seeing people diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes at a younger age — as early as 14 years old.
"This is a warning sign that Type 2 diabetes is no longer a disease affecting only adults, but is now also affecting teenagers and young people," he said during the oral question session in the Dewan Rakyat.
Dzulkefly was responding to Datuk Dr Richard Rapu (GPS-Betong), who asked about the latest statistics on non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and the effectiveness of the War on Sugar campaign.
The minister said data from the National Diabetes Registry showed that new cases among people aged below 30 had increased from 1,926 in 2024 to 1,932 in 2025, with 660 new cases already recorded as of June this year.
He said the country was facing a silent pandemic of NCDs that claimed about 94,000 lives annually — more than twice the number of deaths recorded during the two-year Covid-19 pandemic.
Dzulkefly said figures from the Health Ministry's National Health Screening Initiative, which screened 347,813 people aged between 18 and 30 in 2025, painted an equally worrying picture.
Of those screened, 11,751, or 3.4 per cent, were found to have elevated blood glucose levels, while 30,170, or 8.8 per cent, recorded high blood pressure readings.
Dzulkefly said the findings showed that a significant number of young Malaysians had risk factors for, or undiagnosed, NCDs without realising it, underscoring the importance of early detection and lifestyle intervention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Transforming Malaysia's school counselling units
Article:
THE
time has come for the school counselling units in Malaysia to reassess their roles and positions. For decades, these departments have often been perceived as spaces reserved for "problem students" rather than platforms for holistic development.
However, contemporary challenges, including rising mental health concerns, bullying, and the pressing demand for employability skills, call for a decisive and strategic repositioning of the Malaysian school counselling unit.
We would like to highlight a 2014 doctoral research project translated into the establishment of a pilot initiative under a social enterprise known as SASTRA Education Development.
This initiative introduced and piloted the Human and Career Development Centre (HCDC) model designed to support the school counselling unit under the Malaysian Education Blueprint 2013–2025 (MEB 2013-2025).
The conceptual model was implemented in selected schools across Malaysia, particularly in Selangor and Kedah, and has successfully engaged school clubs, societies, uniform units, government agencies, non-profit organisations, corporations, universities, individuals, and social enterprises within Malaysia.
The project was mainly supported by the "SASTRA Water Bottle Project".
This effort has yielded a reduction in the stigma associated with counselling services, a focus on career development pathways, employability skills among students, and more effective interventions within vulnerable school communities.
SASTRA's core modules, including Social entrepreneurship programmes, were also introduced as part of this career development framework.
The model has been shared with organisations in Scotland, New Zealand, the Philippines, Cambodia, and India to tackle global reskilling challenges.
It has also been validated for refugee schools under the UNHCR in Malaysia, proving to be a valuable reform tool for educational institutions. Additionally, this effort was recognised by the President of the Malaysian University of Science and Technology (MUST).
With significant bullying issues prevalent in schools, it is essential to transform Malaysian school counselling in accordance with the Anti-Bullying Act of 2026 (Act 876).
We emphasise the importance of enhancing career guidance and reskilling pathways, in addition to school counselling, which aligns with the HCDC model.
The current debate is no longer about whether school counselling services should evolve; instead, it is about whether the educational system can afford not to adapt.
Recent legal and policy developments have intensified the need to transform school counselling.
The Anti-Bullying Act 2026 (Act 876) creates a comprehensive framework for preventing and managing bullying, including an Anti-Bullying Tribunal empowered to order remedies such as compensation up to RM250,000, public apologies and mandated counselling.
Section 7 of the Act requires educational institutions to provide child-sensitive reporting channels and establish dedicated prevention and management committees.
Within this architecture, professional counsellors are central to the Act's rehabilitative approach: they deliver tribunal-ordered or mandatory counselling for perpetrators and parents and provide continuous psychosocial support to victims.
The Education Act 1996 (Act 550) empowers the Education Ministry to place qualified counsellors in schools and to drive holistic, continuous psychosocial interventions at the institutional level.
It's time to revisit the Education Act 1996, based on the current and future needs aligned with the Anti-Bullying and HCDC Model.
Within this statutory architecture, schools and educational institutions governed by the Education Act 1996 must establish dedicated anti-bullying committees.
Qualified counsellors play a central, proactive, and restorative role.
They are expected to lead early identification of bullying, facilitate safe and confidential reporting by students, coordinate multi-stakeholder case conferences, and design structured recovery plans for both victims and perpetrators.
This marks a clear shift from viewing counsellors as peripheral support staff to recognising them as key officers in the institutional response to bullying and student protection.
In parallel, the Safe School Guidelines and Student Protection Policy impose clear obligations for immediate mental health interventions.
Counsellors are required to conduct screenings, implement follow-ups, and ensure continuous monitoring of at-risk students.
These frameworks collectively redefine counselling services as an essential pillar of student protection and well-being.
As Malaysia navigates an increasingly complex economic landscape, schools must equip students not only with academic knowledge but also with adaptive skills for future employment.
By implementing the HCDC model at the forefront and aligning the counselling services within the model offers a viable solution as it enables stronger collaboration with parents, communities and the private sector.
The Advisory Committee of the Student Experience and Affairs Department (SEAD) at the Malaysia University of Science and Technology (MUST) is preparing to collectively address the growing issues in Malaysian school counselling units with various stakeholders, including social enterprises, and how it can further support the HCDC model in the national school systems.
Such an approach aligns with the broader national agenda of producing resilient and future-ready citizens.
This initiative should be welcomed at the national level.
* Dr Ananda Devan
is a Learning and Development Transformer, the Brainchild of the HCDC model, Chair of SASTRA Hub, and
Dr Kamini Shanmugaiah
is a Legal Scholar, Head of School (School of Law Enforcement), Malaysia University of Science and Technology (MUST), and an Advocate of the Anti-Bullying Movement.
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Europe Inc heads into strongest earnings season in years, but AI gap persists
Article:
Reuters
LONDON/GDANSK: European companies are heading for their strongest earnings season in more than three years, but investors remain concerned that the region lacks enough artificial intelligence (AI)-powered growth engines to keep pace with the United States.
Second-quarter profits of European blue-chip companies are expected to grow by 15.3 per cent on average, the most since the last quarter of 2022, according to LSEG I/B/E/S data.
Much of that, however, reflects an expected surge in energy company earnings driven by higher crude prices due to the Iran war. Earnings at US companies are forecast to grow 23.7 per cent on average.
Excluding energy, the gap is starker. Non-energy companies in Europe's STOXX 600 index are forecast to report an average six per cent increase in earnings in the quarter, while their S&P 500 counterparts are expected to deliver 19.6 per cent growth, the LSEG I/B/E/S data shows.
The gap is likely to narrow over time, said Jitania Kandhari, deputy chief investment officer of the solutions and multi-asset group at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.
"There will still be a gap next year because the U.S. has very strong, powerful AI earnings and that will continue, but there will be some narrowing where Europe will pick up," she said.
ALL ABOUT GUIDANCE
Others are more sceptical, with European countries struggling with sluggish economic growth.
"For Europe to really start performing, you need some sort of a catalyst, something similar to what we saw last year with the German fiscal stimulus that we just haven't seen yet," said Nataliia Lipikhina, head of EMEA equity strategy at JPMorgan Private Bank, which prefers US and emerging markets.
As second-quarter earnings expectations are already largely reflected in valuations, investors are likely to focus more on what companies say about demand and profits into 2027 than on the results, reporting of which kicks off in earnest next week.
Offering an early glimpse of the AI opportunities for European companies, ASML — the world's biggest supplier of chip-making equipment — raised its 2026 sales forecasts on Wednesday after handily beating earnings expectations in the second quarter.
DIVERGING SECTORS
But it's not all positive. Christoph Berger, CIO Equity Europe at Allianz Global Investors, said higher energy prices have hurt consumer sentiment, adding pressure on sectors such as autos that are already facing weaker demand in China.
Drugmaker Novartis, Italian lender UniCredit, software heavyweight SAP and Volkswagen report earnings next week, all of which could offer clues on the health of the European corporate sector.
Tech and AI will be a key focus, even though Europe lacks the concentration of memory chipmakers or so-called hyperscalers that have driven earnings growth in the United States. Still, Berger notes that "there's a lot of AI-related infrastructure investments. This does help many European industrials".
"We see also contribution and growth from the industrial sectors, ... names which are related to the AI infrastructure topic, also from technology," he said, pointing to semiconductors.
Expectations remain high as investors are unlikely to reward AI-linked companies simply for meeting forecasts, said Martin Frandsen, portfolio manager at Principal Asset Management. They will need to deliver strong messages, he said.
"It's probably not enough to get in line. It's probably not even enough just to get ahead."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Businessman loses RM1.25mil  to cryptocurrency scam
Article:
IPOH: A businessman has lost RM1.25 million after falling victim to a cryptocurrency investment scam that promised lucrative returns.
Perak police chief Datuk Mohd Alwi Zainal Abidin said the police received a report from the victim on July 10.
He said initial investigations found that the scam began in January last year when the victim was introduced to several individuals offering a cryptocurrency investment scheme with promises of high returns.
"To convince the victim, several briefing sessions were held before he agreed to participate in the investment.
"Between March 28 and April 17, 2025, the victim made several digital asset transfers to digital wallets as instructed by the suspects," he said in a statement today.
Mohd Alwi said the victim did not receive the promised returns and was unable to withdraw his investment capital, leading him to lodge a police report after realising he had been cheated.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, which carries a penalty of between one and 10 years' imprisonment, whipping, and a fine upon conviction.
Mohd Alwi advised the public to be wary of investment schemes that promise extraordinary profits or unrealistic fixed returns.
"Members of the public are urged to conduct background checks on companies, platforms and individuals offering investment opportunities and to seek advice from the relevant authorities before making any investment decisions.
"The public is also advised to avoid transferring money or digital assets to individuals or accounts whose authenticity cannot be verified," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops bust alleged online scam syndicate, arrest 18 Chinese citizens
Article:
IPOH: Police have arrested 18 Chinese citizens in two raids here for allegedly operating an online scam syndicate.
Perak police chief Datuk Mohd Alwi Zainal Abidin said the suspects, all men, were detained during operations conducted by the district police headquarters on Monday.
"The first raid at an apartment in Laluan Ipoh Permai 3, Apartment Ipoh Hill Permai, led to the arrest of 10 Chinese citizens, while another eight were detained at a house in Laluan Tasek Timur 5, Taman Tasek Indra.
"Police seized various electronic devices, including laptops, mobile phones and other equipment believed to have been used in online scam operations," he said in a statement today.
Alwi said the total value of the seized items is estimated at RM78,000.
Initial forensic analysis indicated the devices were suspected of being used to facilitate online fraud involving the misuse of financial instruments and bank accounts belonging to third parties.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 and Section 120B of the Penal Code for cheating and criminal conspiracy, as well as Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/63 for entering Malaysia without valid travel documents or overstaying.
"The suspects and all seized items have been taken to the Ipoh district police headquarters for further investigation," he added.
Alwi said police view online scam activities involving the abuse of technology and the misuse of financial accounts seriously.
He added that enforcement operations would continue to be intensified to curb commercial crime and dismantle scam syndicates.
He urged the public to remain vigilant when conducting online transactions, avoid offers promising unrealistic returns, and never disclose banking details or transfer money to unverified parties.
Those with information on commercial crime activities are urged to contact the nearest police station or the National Scam Response Centre via 997.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US imposes 25pct tariff on some goods from Brazil
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US plans to impose a 25 per cent tariff on certain imports from Brazil under Section 301 of the Trade Act, US Trade Representative (USTR) Jamieson Greer said late on Wednesday.
The action follows a year-long probe which concluded that Brazilian policies on digital trade, tariffs, intellectual property, ethanol access and deforestation burden US commerce, USTR said in a statement.
Reuters reported a day before that Brazil was bracing for the US to impose a new 25 per cent tariff on thousands of imports from the South American country after months of intensive but largely unproductive negotiations, citing three people familiar with the matter.
"Today's action is necessary to address these unfair trade practices to ensure American workers and companies can compete on a level playing field," Greer said.
"Extensive negotiations with Brazil over the past year have not resolved these issues," though the US is open to further talks, he said.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the Brazilian government had "not negotiated with the US in good faith" and that Brazil's President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva had "put his own ego ahead of making a deal for the welfare of the Brazilian people."
Brazil would be the first country targeted under US President Donald Trump's new tariff strategy which relies on Section 301 of US trade law, a provision that authorises investigations into alleged unfair trade practices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PM directs Mardi to speed up development of high-yield, drought-resistant padi varieties
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (Mardi) has been directed to accelerate the development of high-yield and drought-resistant padi varieties amid concerns over the impact of El Nino on food production.
Agriculture and Food Security Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim issued the directive during a national food security meeting yesterday.
He said Mardi had been instructed to intensify its research, particularly into padi varieties capable of producing significantly higher yields per hectare.
"The Prime Minister directed Mardi to step up efforts to develop new varieties, particularly by studying the yields achieved per hectare in China, which are considerably higher.
"Mardi has been instructed to further intensify its research," he told the Dewan Rakyat.
Mohamad was responding to Bakri Jamaluddin (PN-Tangga Batu), who asked about the government's preparations for El Nino and its expected impact on agriculture and national food security.
Mohamad said Mardi had developed several padi varieties, including the newly introduced MR333 Menora, as well as MR297, MR315, MR307, MR284 and MRQ76.
He said the institute had also developed the drought-resistant MR380, MR381 and MR382 varieties.
The government is also expanding the use of smart irrigation monitoring and drip irrigation systems to reduce water wastage and distribute water according to actual crop requirements.
Other measures include adopting alternative water management methods, scheduling irrigation more efficiently in major padi-growing areas and providing pumps to draw water from nearby rivers during dry spells.
Mohamad said RM38.53 million in assistance had been disbursed to 55,058 padi farmers affected by drought as of June 30, covering about 16,933ha in northern and eastern Peninsular Malaysia.
He said the government would continue providing assistance following natural disasters, subject to its financial capacity, while ensuring national rice reserves remained sufficient for about six months.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Full report on fatal grenade blast at Hobart Camp expected on July 23
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The probe into the grenade blast that killed two soldiers at Hobart Camp in Kedah last month has yet to be concluded.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Khaled Nordin told the Dewan Rakyat that the Defence Ministry is expecting a complete report from the army headquarters on July 23, 2026.
Khaled was responding to a question from Datuk Mohd Isam Mohd Isa (BN-Tampin), who asked for the cause of the fatal incident and the immediate steps taken to strengthen training safety standard operating procedures (SOPs).
He said a Board of Inquiry had convened a day after the incident on June 17, and the investigation report had since gone through reviews at the 2nd Division Headquarters and the Army Western Field Command Headquarters.
The ministry received the report from the Western Field Command on July 13, but a review by army headquarters found that additional witnesses needed to be recalled and new testimony possibly obtained.
"It is too early to determine the actual cause of the incident before the investigation is fully concluded," he said, adding that the ministry had called for the investigation and report to be expedited.
As an immediate measure, Khaled said the army had carried out a comprehensive special inspection of all remaining grenades in the possession of 1 Squadron to ensure none were damaged.
Army chief General Datuk Azhan Md Othman had also directed that all grenade-throwing exercises be suspended temporarily until the investigation was completed.
Khaled outlined four longer-term safety improvements based on the Board of Inquiry's preliminary recommendations.
The first involves enhanced monitoring at firing ranges through the installation of CCTV cameras, body cameras for After Action Review (AAR) purposes, improved sight lines, and observer mirrors at control towers.
The second proposes the use of eco-friendly training hand grenades for dry training exercises that replicate the weight and feel of live grenades, giving personnel hands-on exposure before handling live ordnance.
The third calls for a formal casting policy for all ammunition and explosive materials, setting a defined operational lifespan and mandating detailed periodic inspections of materials that have reached or are approaching that threshold.
The fourth requires all units to conduct physical health and mental readiness assessments of officers and members before they participate in training, with particular attention to those with health records flagged for monitoring.
On July 5, Khaled said the
full investigation report
on the explosion was expected to be submitted to the ministry by mid-month.
Meanwhile, Armed Forces chief General Tan Sri Malek Razak Sulaiman said on July 7 that the armed forces are reviewing their
training SOPs
to strengthen safety measures and facilities while ensuring operational readiness following two incidents at Hobart Camp.
On June 16,
two soldiers were killed
in a grenade blast during a training exercise.
Less than two weeks later, on June 29, two soldiers suffered leg injuries in a live-firing exercise at the same camp.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Miri woman loses RM1.39m after clicking fake cake ad on social media
Article:
MIRI, July 16 — A 43-year-old woman lost RM1,389,132 after falling victim to an elaborate scam that began with an online cake advertisement on social media.
Miri police chief ACP Mohd Farhan Lee Abdullah said the Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID) received a report on Wednesday on unauthorised withdrawals from the victim’s company bank accounts.
The victim shared that after browsing the menu, she had clicked on a link provided in the advertisement to place an order.
This caused her mobile phone screen to go blank and become unusable.
After checking with the bank at around 10am on Wednesday, she discovered that 19 unauthorised fund transfers had been made from two of her company bank accounts to 15 different bank accounts the previous day, resulting in losses totalling RM1,389,132.
The case is being investigated under Section 4(1) of the Computer Crimes Act 1997 for unauthorised access with intent to commit or facilitate commission of further offence, which carries a maximum fine of RM150,000, imprisonment of up to 10 years, or both upon conviction.
Police advised the public to remain vigilant against phishing scams, particularly fake links circulated through social media, as clicking such links may result in malicious applications being installed or sensitive banking information being compromised.
Members of the public can immediately contact the National Scam Response Centre (NSRC) by dialling 997 if they have just transferred money to a suspected mule account, as well as to verify suspicious bank accounts and phone numbers through the Semak Mule platform before making any financial transactions. — The Borneo Post

Title: Govt to pilot affordable MediAsas health insurance plan this month ahead of nationwide rollout in 2027, says deputy minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 16 — The Base Medical and Health Insurance/Takaful (MHIT) Plan, or MediAsas, will begin its pilot programme by the end of this month, as part of the government’s RESET strategy to tackle long-term private healthcare cost inflation, the Dewan Rakyat was told today.
Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong said the pilot programme would pave the way for the plan’s full implementation nationwide in January 2027.
“MediAsas aims to provide more affordable and sustainable medical coverage for the people in the long term. This will allow more Malaysians to access private healthcare protection,” he said during the question-and-answer session.
He was responding to a question from Pang Hok Liong (PH-Labis), who asked the Finance Minister to state the timeline for introducing regulations to ensure insurance companies control or reduce health insurance premiums that are placing a burden on the public.
Liew said the sharp rise in MHIT premiums in 2024 was driven by a surge in claims following Covid-19, as well as the postponement of premium rate adjustments by insurance and takaful operators during the pandemic.
Responding to a supplementary question from Datuk Mohd Suhaimi Abdullah (PN-Langkawi) on insurance companies’ practice of closing legacy portfolios, which was alleged to discriminate against senior citizens, Liew said the matter was being handled by Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM).
He said the issue had been flagged and was previously under the attention of a Parliamentary Special Select Committee.
He said BNM would introduce a no look-back policy under the MediAsas plan to ensure a fairer insurance market.
“Through this no look-back policy, people who reach a certain age will continue to have coverage even if they develop new illnesses, ensuring their policies are not terminated and they can continue to make claims,” he said. — Bernama

Title: More than 3,600 Sabah citizenship applications still pending, Dewan Rakyat told
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 16 — As of May 31 this year, 3,640 citizenship applications in Sabah were still pending, while 10 had been approved and citizenship certificates issued to the successful applicants.
Deputy Home Minister Datuk Seri Shamsul Anuar Nasarah said that, for late birth registration applications in Sabah, 2,659 applications had been approved, while 611 were still being processed.
